subject: ap-government
theme: The U.S. Constitution was designed to balance democratic self-governance with institutional safeguards against tyranny — using federalism, separation of powers, and checks and balances to control faction and protect natural rights.
core_concepts: Natural rights theory (Locke): people possess unalienable rights to life, liberty, and property — governments are instituted to protect these rights and derive legitimacy only from the consent of the governed. The Declaration of Independence embeds this framework.

Social contract: individuals sacrifice some freedom to government in exchange for security and order; government that fails to protect rights can be legitimately altered or abolished (Declaration, Federalist No. 10).

Popular sovereignty vs. limited government: the Constitution grounds authority in 'We the People' but restricts what government may do — enumerated powers, reserved powers (10th Amendment), and the Bill of Rights all constrain federal authority.

Federalism divides sovereignty: enumerated powers vest in the national government; reserved powers remain with states; the necessary and proper clause and Commerce Clause allow implied powers — McCulloch v. Maryland (1819) established broad implied-powers doctrine.

Separation of powers + checks and balances (Federalist No. 51): each branch has distinct powers and can check the others — 'auxiliary precautions' beyond popular elections guard against tyranny; federalism adds a second layer of vertical security.

Federalist vs. Anti-Federalist debate: Federalists (Madison, Hamilton, Jay) favored the large republic as faction's cure; Anti-Federalists (Brutus/Yates) feared unlimited federal power — their demands produced the Bill of Rights.

Democracy models: participatory (direct citizen engagement), pluralist (competing group interests), and elite (qualified leadership) describe different theories of who should govern — College Board tests recognition of all three.

Articles of Confederation failures — no taxing power, no executive, no federal courts, supermajority requirement, state currency chaos — made Shays' Rebellion the crisis that catalyzed the Constitutional Convention.
exam_tip: AP exam questions on Unit 1 almost always use excerpts from Federalist No. 10, Brutus No. 1, Federalist No. 51, or the Declaration — know each document's core argument and how they respond to each other; be ready to connect them to specific constitutional provisions (necessary and proper clause, 10th Amendment, separation of powers).
